explicit_text: I remember when Viola Liuzzo got killed over there in Alabama. She was shot on the highway while driving back from a meeting, after having given someone a ride home. Her death was a big shock to us, and we held a mass meeting in her honor in Lowndes County, Alabama. It was a dangerous time, and her killing showed how violent things were getting.
implicit_text: I remember when a civil rights activist got killed over there in Alabama. She was shot on the highway while driving back from a meeting, after having given someone a ride home. Her death was a big shock to us, and we held a mass meeting in Lowndes County, Alabama. It was a dangerous time, and her killing showed how violent things were getting.

explicit_text: Alabama was a tough place to be during the civil rights movement, especially in Lowndes County where we went after Viola Liuzzo was killed. The people there were poor, and the food was rough, like parched corn and greasy fatback. We faced a lot of hostility from the local white folks, who even came armed to try to disrupt our meetings. It was a place where you had to be ready to defend yourself, but we kept pushing on.
implicit_text: That state was a tough place to be during the civil rights movement, especially in Lowndes County where we went after the activist was killed. The people there were poor, and the food was rough, like parched corn and greasy fatback. We faced a lot of hostility from the local white folks, who even came armed to try to disrupt our meetings. It was a place where you had to be ready to defend yourself, but we kept pushing on.

explicit_text: I was sitting up in Atlanta when we heard about Viola Liuzzo's death in Alabama. The SNCC office there called us for help, so me and Cynthia Washington jumped in a car and headed down south. Atlanta was kind of our base, and from there we coordinated efforts and responded quickly to events happening in Alabama. It was a hub for organizing and support during those times.
implicit_text: I was sitting up in the major southern city when we heard about the activist's death in Alabama. The civil rights group office there called us for help, so me and Cynthia Washington jumped in a car and headed down south. That city was kind of our base, and from there we coordinated efforts and responded quickly to events happening in Alabama. It was a hub for organizing and support during those times.

explicit_text: I was part of SNCC, and when Viola Liuzzo was killed, we jumped into action. SNCC was always about sticking together and giving people cover, especially in places like Alabama where the danger was real. We organized mass meetings, armed ourselves for protection, and made sure people could get home safely. SNCC was more than an organization; it was a family fighting for progress.
implicit_text: I was part of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, and when the activist was killed, we jumped into action. That group was always about sticking together and giving people cover, especially in places like Alabama where the danger was real. We organized mass meetings, armed ourselves for protection, and made sure people could get home safely. It was more than an organization; it was a family fighting for progress.

explicit_text: Cynthia Washington was with me when we got the call from the SNCC office in Atlanta about Viola Liuzzo's death. We jumped in a car together and drove down to Alabama to help out. She carried a dagger on her belt, and I had a pocket knife; we were ready to face whatever came. Cynthia was a fellow fighter and companion in those dangerous times.
implicit_text: Cynthia Washington was with me when we got the call from the civil rights group office in Atlanta about the activist's death. We jumped in a car together and drove down to Alabama to help out. She carried a dagger on her belt, and I had a pocket knife; we were ready to face whatever came. Cynthia was a fellow fighter and companion in those dangerous times.

explicit_text: Lowndes County, Alabama, was way out in the country, and that's where we held a mass meeting after Viola Liuzzo was killed. The people there were poor and tough, and the local white folks came armed to try to disrupt us, thinking Martin Luther King Jr. was coming. We were ready though, with shotguns and rifles, and we stood our ground. It was a hard place, but it was important for the movement.
implicit_text: Lowndes County, way out in the country, was where we held a mass meeting after the activist was killed. The people there were poor and tough, and the local white folks came armed to try to disrupt us, thinking Martin Luther King Jr. was coming. We were ready though, with shotguns and rifles, and we stood our ground. It was a hard place, but it was important for the movement.

explicit_text: Stokely Carmichael was the main speaker at the mass meeting in Lowndes County after Viola Liuzzo's death. He stood firm inside the church while armed white folks tried to intimidate us outside. Stokely also came to my defense once at a conference in Atlanta when some middle-class folks were making fun of me. Later, he married Miriam Makeba and moved to Africa, and I never saw him again.
implicit_text: The main speaker at the mass meeting in Lowndes County after the activist's death was a prominent civil rights leader. He stood firm inside the church while armed white folks tried to intimidate us outside. He also came to my defense once at a conference in Atlanta when some middle-class folks were making fun of me. Later, he married a famous South African singer and moved to Africa, and I never saw him again.

explicit_text: There was a rumor that Martin Luther King Jr. was coming to the mass meeting in Lowndes County, and that made the local white folks come out in droves, armed and ready to cause trouble. But it was actually Stokely Carmichael speaking, and we were prepared to defend ourselves. King was a big figure in the movement, and his presence or even the thought of it could stir up a lot of tension.
implicit_text: There was a rumor that the iconic civil rights leader was coming to the mass meeting in Lowndes County, and that made the local white folks come out in droves, armed and ready to cause trouble. But it was actually the other prominent speaker, and we were prepared to defend ourselves. That leader was a big figure in the movement, and his presence or even the thought of it could stir up a lot of tension.

explicit_text: I used to drive around with a Dougherty County, Georgia tag on my car when I was out on the backroads at night. That was risky because people knew I was a freedom rider, and those lonesome highways were where you could get caught. I was always scared but made sure to carry something for protection. It was a tense time, but I managed to get through it.
implicit_text: I used to drive around with a license plate from a southwestern Georgia county on my car when I was out on the backroads at night. That was risky because people knew I was a freedom rider, and those lonesome highways were where you could get caught. I was always scared but made sure to carry something for protection. It was a tense time, but I managed to get through it.

explicit_text: Like with Dougherty County, I also had a Fulton County, Georgia tag on my car when I was driving around at night during the movement. It was dangerous because the backroads were watched, and people knew I was involved in the freedom rides. I always carried a pistol or something for protection, but I tried not to put others at risk. Those were scary times, but we kept moving.
implicit_text: Like with that southwestern Georgia county, I also had a license plate from a northern Georgia county on my car when I was driving around at night during the movement. It was dangerous because the backroads were watched, and people knew I was involved in the freedom rides. I always carried a pistol or something for protection, but I tried not to put others at risk. Those were scary times, but we kept moving.

explicit_text: Charles Sherrod was a big help to me when I first got involved with SNCC. He took me into the back room at the SNCC office and helped me learn by giving me books and tutoring me on what I didn’t understand. He spent hours with me, and thanks to him, I got confident enough to speak at conferences. He was a mentor and a friend during those early days.
implicit_text: A key civil rights leader was a big help to me when I first got involved with the student group. He took me into the back room at the office and helped me learn by giving me books and tutoring me on what I didn’t understand. He spent hours with me, and thanks to him, I got confident enough to speak at conferences. He was a mentor and a friend during those early days.

explicit_text: My first big conference was at Union Theological Seminary up in Atlanta, where I got up and made a speech. I was nervous and got laughed at by some middle-class folks, which made me cry. But Stokely Carmichael stood up for me there, grabbed the microphone, and shut them down. That moment pushed me to work harder on my speaking and learning.
implicit_text: My first big conference was at a theological seminary in New York City, where I got up and made a speech. I was nervous and got laughed at by some middle-class folks, which made me cry. But the prominent civil rights leader stood up for me there, grabbed the microphone, and shut them down. That moment pushed me to work harder on my speaking and learning.

explicit_text: Miriam Makeba married Stokely Carmichael, and after that, he went to Africa and stayed there for a long time. I never saw Stokely again after he left. Miriam was part of that connection that took Stokely away from the movement here, and it marked the end of an era for us.
implicit_text: A famous South African singer married the prominent civil rights leader, and after that, he went to Africa and stayed there for a long time. I never saw him again after he left. She was part of that connection that took him away from the movement here, and it marked the end of an era for us.
